title: What Happens When You Give Teenagers the Keys to Your Museum?
content:
Here’s a thought experiment: What if your museum handed over its TikTok account to a bunch of teenagers—no filters, no approvals, just… full access?

If your heart just skipped a beat, you’re not alone.

Most cultural institutions would call that a PR risk. But at the Stedelijk Museum in Amsterdam, it’s business as usual. In fact, it’s part of a radical (and remarkably successful) strategy for youth engagement that’s been quietly reshaping the museum from the inside out.

At the Museums & Public Programs Summit Olmo Garcia Koel, Youth Programme Coordinator at the Stedelijk Museum shared this extraordinary initiative.

The Problem with Most Youth Programs<

creator: Manual Charr

title: Stress-Free Museum Visits? How ‘Quiet Hours’ Are Changing the Game
content:
Museums have long been sanctuaries of art, history, and culture, offering visitors a chance to step away from the hustle and bustle of daily life. But for some people, traditional museum visits can feel overwhelming—the crowds, bright lights, and background noise can turn what should be a calming experience into a stressful one.

To address this, many museums are introducing ‘Quiet Hours’ or ‘Quiet Visits.’ These dedicated periods provide a calmer, more sensory-friendly environment, making museums more inclusive and welcoming.

What Are ‘Quiet Hours’ in Museums?

‘Quiet Hours’ are specific times set aside by museums when sensory stimuli are intentionally reduced. This can include dimming bright lights, lowering or turning off background sounds, limiting visitor numbers, and providing quiet spaces for rest. These adjustments are designed to create a more comfortable environment for neurodivergent individuals, such as those with autism or sensory processing dif
snippet: Museums have long been sanctuaries of art, history, and culture, offering visitors a chance to step away from the hustle and bustle of daily life. But for some people, traditional museum visits can fe
creator: Jim Richardson

title: Five Ideas Museums Can Use to Transform Their Social Media
content:
Here’s a question worth asking: What does a cardboard banana have to do with digital transformation at a national museum?

The answer, it turns out, reveals something fundamental about how cultural institutions can succeed online. Megan Jones, Digital Content Manager at National Museums Scotland recently shared with MuseumNext how her team have spent a year doing something most museums struggle with: giving themselves permission to experiment.

This isn’t a story about viral hits or massive budgets. It’s about building a culture where trying something new matters more than getting everything perfect. Where a curator holding a prop banana can teach you more about audience connection than a doz

creator: Manual Charr

title: How Manchester Museum Turned a Spider Crab Into a Social Media Powerhouse
content:
If you stroll down Oxford Road in Manchester, you may notice an eight-legged sentinel lurking behind glass—looming, slightly menacing, and, as it turns out, unexpectedly charismatic. This Japanese spider crab, long a favourite for startling passing students, is now far more than a static display specimen. As Connor Murphy explained in his presentation at the Museums & Social Media Summit, “this guy is very much the star of the show… and now star of our social media as well.”

That sentence might seem unremarkable—except the star in question is not a curator, or an educator, or a celebrity collaborator. It’s a crab. A taxidermied one.

And somehow, this crab has become one of the most effective digital engagem

title: How to Create a Viral Video for a Museum
content:
What does it really take to create a viral video for a museum? Is it about the object on screen, the quick editing, the platform, or the storytelling? To uncover the reality behind museum social media success, we asked social media experts at London Museum, Stedelijk Museum Amsterdam, and The National Gallery to share the strategies that have helped their videos reach millions.

Their insights reveal that viral museum videos are not accidents. They are crafted through authenticity, curiosity, and the courage to show museums as human, creative places. If you are wondering how to create a viral video for a museum, here is what the sector’s most innovative teams want you to know.

Start With a Story That Sparks Curiosity

title: English Heritage’s Shout Out Loud: Supporting Creative Skills for Young People
content:
If you ask most people what heritage organisations do, they will probably point you toward the past, stories carved in stone, artefacts under glass, timelines printed on crisp exhibition panels. But ask Dr Kirsty McCarrison, Senior Partnerships and Participation Manager at English Heritage, and she will flip that idea on its head.

Her argument is simple: heritage is not just backwards-facing. It is a lens for making sense of the present and a surprising launchpad for young people imagining their futures.

Through Shout Out Loud, English Heritage’s national youth engagement programme, she is helping young people not only interpret history but also add their own creative layer

creator: Manual Charr

title: How One Museum Turned a Podcast Into a Sold-Out Live Show
content:
If you want to understand how The Object, a quietly loved podcast from the Minneapolis Institute of Art (MIA) grew into a theatre-filling live event, complete with celebrity guests, audience quizzes, and a small army of superfans, you have to start with a confession: the original concept was terrible.

Years ago, MIA’s director announced that the museum needed a podcast. Writer and museum staffer Tim Gihring gamely grabbed a curator, stood them in a gallery, and switched on a microphone. The result was… earnest, but lifeless. A second attempt in a bar produced something livelier, but for all the wrong reasons.

So Gihring fell back on his background as a journalist and rebu

creator: Manual Charr

title: Inside V&A East Storehouse, A Radical Rethink of What a Museum Can Be
content:
Walk into the V&A East Storehouse and the first thing that hits you is the scale. Half a million objects sit in a vast former Olympic press building, from eighteenth century paintings to netsuke small enough to balance on a fingertip. Decades of curatorial care are visible everywhere. Lacquer sits in climate controlled cabinets, textiles stretch across rolling racks, and fragile objects sleep in precisely engineered housings.

But the real story here is not storage, it is access. Because the team behind V&A East has spent eight years building something far more ambitious than a store. They have built a new system of cultural entry, designed around the needs and imaginations of young people in East London.

And they did it with one provocative question in mind: what if a stored collection coul

creator: Manual Charr

title: Designing for the Planet: Lessons on Sustainable Exhibition Design
content:
Sustainable exhibition design is no longer an optional enhancement, it is an expectation shared by audiences, funders, and cultural workers who increasingly recognise museums as civic actors in the climate crisis. As the sector experiments with new materials, new workflows, and new value systems, pioneering organisations are showing what sustainable practice looks like in action.

Drawing on presentations from Documenta, Miraikan and Young V&A at the Green Museums Summit, this article distils the key lessons emerging from real-world projects that are redefining how exhibitions are conceived, built and maintained for a post-carbon future.

Treat Sustainability as a Holistic Exhibition Framework

The strongest lesson from Documenta is that sustainability cannot be bolted on. It must be embedded
snippet: Sustainable exhibition design is no longer an optional enhancement, it is an expectation shared by audiences, funders, and cultural workers who increasingly recognise museums as civic actors in the cl
creator: Manual Charr

title: Tips for Climate Change Storytelling in Museums
content:
Climate change is complex, emotionally charged, and often overwhelming. Museums, however, have the power to translate this global issue into stories people can feel, understand, and act upon.

Recent presentations at the Green Museums Summit from NASA, the Royal Ontario Museum and Concordia Art Space—offer a fresh set of insights into how museums can communicate climate issues with clarity, empathy and imagination.

Start With People, Not With “Climate Change”

At the Royal Ontario Museum, Soren Brothers observes that the phrase “climate change” alone can close doors rather than open them. For some visitors, the topic feels too political; for others, it triggers anxiety or fatigue. Beginning with climate science or warnings may unintentionally alienate the very people museums hope to enga
